flashcards.back: Any waste generated during diagnosis, treatment, immunization of humans/animals, or in related research activities. Includes materials with potentially infectious substances.

flashcards.front: List the 4 major categories of BMW under WHO classification.
flashcards.back: 1) Infectious waste (cultures, swabs, contaminated materials) 2) Pathological waste (tissues, organs, body parts) 3) Sharps (needles, blades, broken glass) 4) Pharmaceutical waste (expired drugs, vaccines)

Waste containing pathogens in sufficient concentration to cause disease. Includes: cultures of microorganisms, pathological waste, waste from isolation wards, contaminated gloves, bandages, and excreta.
Human or animal tissues, organs, body parts, fetuses, blood, and body fluids. Includes recognizable body parts and anatomical waste from surgeries, autopsies, and animal houses.

flashcards.front: What are sharps and why are they dangerous?
flashcards.back: Items that can cause cuts or puncture wounds: needles, syringes, scalpels, blades, broken glass, broken ampoules. Dangerous due to potential for injury and transmission of blood-borne pathogens like HIV, Hepatitis B & C.
Expired, unused, or contaminated medicines and drugs. Includes antibiotics, cytotoxic drugs, vaccines, and discarded items from manufacturing. Requires special disposal due to chemical toxicity.

Waste containing substances with genotoxic properties, capable of causing genetic mutation, cancer, or birth defects. Includes chemotherapy drugs, their metabolites, and materials contaminated with them.

flashcards.front: What is chemical waste in BMW context?
flashcards.back: Waste containing chemical substances used in healthcare: disinfectants, solvents, heavy metals (mercury in thermometers), batteries, photographic chemicals, formaldehyde, and laboratory reagents.

flashcards.front: List major sources of biomedical waste.
flashcards.back: 1) Hospitals (multi-specialty, nursing homes) 2) Clinics & dispensaries 3) Diagnostic laboratories 4) Blood banks 5) Research institutions 6) Animal research facilities 7) Pharmaceutical industries 8) Funeral homes & mortuaries

flashcards.front: What is radioactive waste in hospitals?
flashcards.back: Waste containing radioactive materials used in diagnosis/treatment: Iodine-131, Cobalt-60, Technetium-99m. Includes contaminated materials from nuclear medicine, radiotherapy departments, and research labs.

flashcards.front: What is general waste in BMW context?
flashcards.back: Non-hazardous waste similar to domestic waste: paper, cardboard, plastics (non-contaminated), food waste, packaging materials. Does not pose biological or chemical hazard. Comprises 75-90% of hospital waste.

flashcards.front: What percentage of hospital waste is hazardous BMW?
flashcards.back: Approximately 10-25% of total hospital waste is hazardous biomedical waste. The remaining 75-90% is general/non-hazardous waste. WHO estimates 15 kg of BMW is generated per bed per month in hospitals.
